revision.text:
[[ja:洪積台地]] {{see also|Plateau|qq=}} [[File:Mikatahara & Iwatahara Relief Map, SRTM-1.jpg|thumb|A relief map of western Shizuoka Prefecture.
[[Mikatahara]] diluvial upland (west) and [[Iwatahara]] diluvial upland (east), with the [[Tenryū River]] between them.]] '''Diluvial upland''' (洪積台地, ''kōseki daichi'', {{ill|English|en|English|qid=Q182|lt=English|1=English}}: diluvial upland) refers to elevated flatlands, such as [[:en:Alluvial fans|Alluvial fans]] and [[:en:deltas|deltas]], formed during the {{ill|Pleistocene|en|Pleistocene|qid=Q25546|lt=Pleistocene|1=Pleistocene}} (Diluvial Epoch) and subsequently uplifted. These uplands are typically small-scale and low in elevation (though exceptions like the [[Makinohara Upland]] can reach altitudes of 200m). Due to good drainage, flat terrain, and solid ground conditions, diluvial uplands are often ideal for construction but unsuitable for [[:en:Rice paddies|Rice paddies]]. They are instead commonly used for [[dry field farming]], [[:en:orchards|orchards]], or [[tea plantations]]. == Overview == The term "diluvial" originates from the concept of the "Great Flood" (as in ''Noah's Flood''), reflecting early geologic interpretations of catastrophic events shaping the earth's surface. Modern geomorphology has moved away from using "diluvial upland," instead referring to these formations simply as "uplands" formed during the last interglacial period and afterward. As of 2017, six out of nine high school geography textbooks in Japan use "upland," while three note the term "diluvial upland" as historical.''Geography,'' September 2017 (Kokon Shoin) {{JAN|4910061550979|qq=}}{{要ページ番号|date=2024th year 2月|qq=}}{{Cite web|url=http://www.teikokushoin.co.jp/teacher/previous_files/changesocial/geo/index.html|title=Changes in Social Studies - Maps and Geography Q3: Why is "diluvial upland" now written as "upland"?|publisher=Teikoku Shoin|accessdate=2024-02-19|archiveurl=https://web.archive.org/web/20180713072917/http://www.teikokushoin.co.jp/teacher/previous_files/changesocial/geo/index.html#q3|archivedate=2018-07-13|qq=}} == Major Diluvial Uplands in Japan == ;[[Nemuro-Kushiro Upland]] (根釧台地) : Spanning 5,000 km² in [[:en:Hokkaidō|Hokkaidō]]’s {{ill|Nemuro Subprefecture|en|Nemuro Subprefecture|qid=Q1151431|lt=Nemuro Subprefecture|1=Nemuro Subprefecture}} and {{ill|Kushiro Subprefecture|en|Kushiro Subprefecture|qid=Q1141563|lt=Kushiro Subprefecture|1=Kushiro Subprefecture}}, this upland lies 100–200m above sea level. It is famous for its volcanic ash layers and grid-like windbreak forests. ;[[Musashino Upland]] (武蔵野台地) : Formed from the [[Kanto Loam]], this upland has fertile, well-drained soils. It was transformed into arable land during the {{ill|Edo period|en|Edo period|qid=Q184963|lt=Edo period|1=Edo period}} with irrigation systems like the {{ill|Tamagawa Aqueduct|en|Tamagawa Aqueduct|qid=Q1014298|lt=Tamagawa Aqueduct|1=Tamagawa Aqueduct}}. While urbanization is prominent, some agricultural areas remain, particularly in parts of {{ill|Saitama Prefecture|en|Saitama Prefecture|qid=Q128186|lt=Saitama Prefecture|1=Saitama Prefecture}}. ;[[Ōmiya Upland]] (大宮台地) : Also derived from Kanto Loam, it lies centrally in Saitama Prefecture, between the Musashino and [[Shimōsa Upland]]. Urbanization has significantly advanced here. ;[[Shimōsa Upland]] (下総台地, or Jōsō Upland) : Encompasses northern {{ill|Chiba Prefecture|en|Chiba Prefecture|qid=Q80011|lt=Chiba Prefecture|1=Chiba Prefecture}}, southern {{ill|Ibaraki Prefecture|en|Ibaraki Prefecture|qid=Q83273|lt=Ibaraki Prefecture|1=Ibaraki Prefecture}}, and parts of eastern Saitama. Formed from Kanto Loam, its sections include the '''Hokusō Upland''' in Chiba, '''Hitachi Upland''' in Ibaraki, and '''Hōshuka Upland''' or '''Kanasugi Upland''' in Saitama. The area boasts some of Japan’s highest agricultural yields, particularly in suburban areas close to Tokyo. ;[[Makinohara Upland]] (牧之原台地) : Located in the southeastern part of [[:en:Enshū|Enshū]], {{ill|Shizuoka Prefecture|en|Shizuoka Prefecture|qid=Q131320|lt=Shizuoka Prefecture|1=Shizuoka Prefecture}}, it is renowned for tea cultivation. ;[[Mikatahara Upland]] (三方原台地) : Found in [[:en:Hamamatsu City|Hamamatsu City]], Shizuoka Prefecture, it is notable as the site of the {{ill|Battle of Mikatagahara|en|Battle of Mikatagahara|qid=Q690464|lt=Battle of Mikatagahara|1=Battle of Mikatagahara}} and for extensive tea farming. ;[[Atsuta Upland]] and [[Gokiso Upland]] (熱田台地・御器所台地) : These uplands form part of the central and eastern areas of [[:en:Nagoya City|Nagoya City]]. ;[[Uemachi Upland]] (上町台地) : Running north-south through central [[:en:Osaka City|Osaka City]], this upland features landmarks like {{ill|Osaka Castle|en|Osaka Castle|qid=Q321242|lt=Osaka Castle|1=Osaka Castle}} and serves as a hub for government offices and residential areas. == References == == See Also == * {{ill|Upland|en|Upland|qid=Q397811|lt=Upland|1=Upland}} * {{ill|Yamanote (urban areas)|ja|山の手|qid=Q1424630|1=Yamanote}} {{Earth's landforms|qq=}} {{Geo-stub|qq=}} {{translated page|ja|洪積台地|version=99321939|comment=Imported full ja history}} [[ja:洪積台地]] {{wikidata link|Q11556488}} [[Category:Plateaus]] [[Category:translated pages with valid en interwikis]] [[Category:Wikidata no short description]]
